name: Proto-Peninsular Arabic
notes: Proto-Peninsular Arabic — abstract ancestor node for Hejazi, Najdi, Gulf, Yemeni, and Omani Arabic. Key shared peninsular features: interdentals often preserved; ق → /q/ or /ɡ/ (Bedouin); conservative consonant system. Emphatic (pharyngealization) spreading — low vowels back to [ɑ]/[ɑː] next to /tˤ dˤ sˤ ðˤ zˤ/ — is declared here as allophone_rules (AR_PEN_EMPH_*) and inherited by all Peninsular descendants. ORTHOGRAPHIC READING BASE: graphemes/positional_graphemes inherit from the modern MSA leaf `ar` (not Classical `arb`), because the modern spoken varieties are written in MSA orthography — so they must read it the way `ar` does: the onset-glide digraphs يَ/وَ (/ja/,/wa/, not diphthongs), the hamza carriers أ/إ as bare /ʔ/ before an explicit harakah, word-final ي/و preferring the long-vowel reading, and pausal tāʾ marbūṭa. Genealogy is unchanged (parent/ancestors still point at Classical); only the data-inheritance base moves. Each variety's own phonological overrides (interdentals, qāf reflex, affrication) sit in its own tables and continue to win over the inherited layer.

sources.author: Watson, J.C.E.
sources.title: The Phonology and Morphology of Arabic

sources.author: Holes, C.
sources.title: Modern Arabic: Structures, Functions, and Varieties

allophone_rules.notes: Emphasis (pharyngealization) spreading: short /a/ backs to [ɑ] when it follows an emphatic consonant /tˤ dˤ sˤ ðˤ (zˤ)/. Watson 2002, The Phonology and Morphology of Arabic (OUP), ch. 'Emphasis', pp. 267-286: pharyngealization spreads bidirectionally within the phonological word, backing low vowels. Shared Peninsular feature; inherited by Hejazi, Najdi, Gulf, Yemeni and Omani via graphemes_base/OVERLAY_BY_ID.

Emphasis spreading (leftward): short /a/ backs to [ɑ] before an adjacent emphatic consonant. Watson 2002, The Phonology and Morphology of Arabic (OUP), ch. 'Emphasis', pp. 267-286: pharyngealization spreads bidirectionally within the phonological word, backing low vowels. Shared Peninsular feature; inherited by Hejazi, Najdi, Gulf, Yemeni and Omani via graphemes_base/OVERLAY_BY_ID.

Emphasis spreading: long /aː/ backs to [ɑː] after an adjacent emphatic. Watson 2002, The Phonology and Morphology of Arabic (OUP), ch. 'Emphasis', pp. 267-286: pharyngealization spreads bidirectionally within the phonological word, backing low vowels. Shared Peninsular feature; inherited by Hejazi, Najdi, Gulf, Yemeni and Omani via graphemes_base/OVERLAY_BY_ID.

Emphasis spreading (leftward): long /aː/ backs to [ɑː] before an adjacent emphatic. Watson 2002, The Phonology and Morphology of Arabic (OUP), ch. 'Emphasis', pp. 267-286: pharyngealization spreads bidirectionally within the phonological word, backing low vowels. Shared Peninsular feature; inherited by Hejazi, Najdi, Gulf, Yemeni and Omani via graphemes_base/OVERLAY_BY_ID.

location.notes: Glottolog's computed centroid for the Arabian Peninsula Arabic family — a summary of a whole clade's range, not a place anyone speaks.

stress.notes: Arabic stress is QUANTITY-SENSITIVE: it falls on a syllable because that syllable is heavy, not because of any orthographic ending, so no ending table can express it. The cascade (Ryding, A Reference Grammar of MSA, CUP 2005, §2.3; Watson, The Phonology and Morphology of Arabic, OUP 2002, ch.3): a superheavy final syllable (CVːC/CVCC) takes the stress (kiˈtaːb); otherwise a heavy penult (CVː/CVC) does (muˈdarris); otherwise the antepenult (ˈmadrasa). max_onset=1 because Arabic onsets are obligatory and hold exactly one consonant — a medial cluster splits, closing the preceding syllable, and that boundary is what makes the penult of mudarris heavy. INPUT CONTRACT: weight is only recoverable from a complete transcription, so this requires diacritized input; an undiacritized skeleton has no vowels to weigh. The registered wikipron gold carries no stress marks, but the benchmark strips stress from both sides by default, so this costs no PER.
